questions.prompt: How do you say 'I am a student' in Croatian?
questions.hint: Think about the first person singular of 'biti'.
questions.explanation: 'Sam' is the first person singular present tense of 'biti' (to be). Ja sam = I am.

questions.prompt: Which greeting is used in the evening?
questions.explanation: Dobra večer = Good evening. Dobro jutro = Good morning. Dobar dan = Good day/afternoon.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'goodbye' in Croatian?
questions.explanation: Doviđenja = goodbye. Hvala = thank you. Molim = please / you're welcome.

questions.prompt: Kako se ___? (What is your name? — formal)
questions.explanation: 'Kako se zovete?' is the formal way to ask someone's name. 'Zovete' is 2nd person plural/formal of 'zvati se'.
'Drago mi je' is the standard expression for 'Nice to meet you.' Drago = dear/pleased.
Kako = how, se zovete = are you called (formal). Literally 'How do you call yourself?'
'Drago mi je' is a fixed expression meaning 'Nice to meet you' or literally 'It is dear to me'.
Dobar dan = Good day (standard daytime greeting). Kako ste = How are you (formal).
Dobro = good (neuter), jutro = morning (neuter noun, so the adjective matches).
'Drago mi je' is the standard expression. Drago = dear/glad, mi = to me, je = is.
Zoveš is the 2nd person singular (informal) of 'zvati se'. Compare with formal: zovete.

questions.prompt: Conjugate 'zvati se' (to be called/named) in the present tense.
questions.correctAnswer: ja se zovem, ti se zoveš, on/ona/ono se zove, mi se zovemo, vi se zovete, oni/one/ona se zovu
questions.hint: This is an -em conjugation verb. Don't forget 'se'!
questions.explanation: Zvati se follows the -em pattern. 'Se' is the reflexive particle and always comes before the verb in this position.
questions.exampleSentence: Kako se zoveš? — Zovem se Ana.
questions.exampleTranslation: What's your name? — My name is Ana.
